name: Cova d'en Moret
best_season: Avoid the hottest hours in summer
access: Own vehicle or La Vileta bus (EMT line 8)
coordinates_utm: 465840 E, 4384345 N (DATUM EUROPEAN 1950) / 465745 E, 4384140 N (DATUM ETRS89)
description: A magnitude IV collapse cave linked to the dissolution of Keuper gypsum (Triassic) at the base of Serra de na Burguesa. First explored in 1956 by the EME group, it was lost and rediscovered in 2011. It features two main halls — the upper hall with a large wall flowstone, and the expansive Blocks Hall with impressive gypsum blocks, flowstones, stalactites, gours, and notably beautiful eccentric formations along the southwest wall. CO2 accumulations in deeper areas require caution.
full_description:
A bit of history

Between 1955 and 1956, the first Mallorcan speleology group, the "Equip Mallorquí d'Espeleologia" (EME), began speleological work in the "Serra de Tramuntana" (GINÉS, 1993). "Cova d'en Moret" was one of the caves this group explored and surveyed, beginning work on June 29, 1956 and finishing on November 4, 1956 (GINARD, 2009), in addition to carrying out biospeleological work. Everything seems to indicate that "Cova d'en Moret" had been missing from the world of speleology for a long time, along with some others the group had worked on. In fact, the existing coordinates of the cave did not help much in locating it, nor did the location map, nor the size of the entrance. Of magnitude IV, it is one of the largest caves in the area and one of the most interesting. The name of the cave perhaps refers to the person who may have found it, since Moret is a surname of Catalan origin, specifically from Girona and Barcelona, so many people carry it on the island. Unconfirmed, there appears to be a path with the same name in the area that may also have influenced the naming.

Rediscovery and exploration

July 9, 2011

J. M. Torres, a visitor to the area, contacted V. Pardo to inform him of the discovery of what he believed was an unrecorded cave. Pardo in turn contacted J. Bermejo, who after some checks and accompanied by V. Pardo went to the cavity, bringing with him the survey of "Cova d'en Moret" to verify whether it was indeed the same cave or an uncatalogued one. After confirming that the cave was the one surveyed by the Equip Mallorquí d'Espeleologia (EME), they took a few photos and left the site.

July 23, 2011

J. Bermejo and R. Minguillón began the survey of the cave with the intention of updating it, having to abandon the work a couple of hours later due to the considerable concentration of CO2 in the deepest part.

August 6, 2011

Again J. Bermejo, this time accompanied by V. Pardo, continued the survey work, taking advantage of a few free hours, and finished the plan of the "Column Hall."

August 10, 2011

J. Bermejo together with G. Herráez dedicated more than two hours to the survey. It is curious — having a cave so close to the city, instead of helping finish the work sooner, at least in this case, is prolonging it excessively.

August 18, 2011

Again J. Bermejo, accompanied by G. Herráez and two of his nephews, headed to the cave with the intention of finishing the survey. R. Minguillón joined the team a little later. Once the objective was achieved — at least that was the feeling, since one never knows — we concluded the day's work after three hours.

Description:

With a NE-SW orientation, this is a collapse cave linked to the dissolution of Keuper gypsum (Triassic) that forms the base of the Serra de na Burguesa, like some of the cavities found in the area of this work.

It is accessed through a small entrance of 0.93 x 0.65 m, descending along a ramp that exceeds 45° at some points with a height of less than fifty centimeters. Although there is an installation at the access entrance for using rope, it is possible to descend safely by using opposition technique.

Upper Hall

Once past the access ramp, we find ourselves in an elongated hall running SE-NW, with dimensions of 12 x 18 x 3 m, whose northeastern side is occupied by a large wall flowstone. We must continue the descent to the bottom of the hall where we will find the access beneath the large flowstone to our right.

In addition to the flowstone, a small rimstone pool in the deepest zone stands out in this hall, as well as the abundance of botryoidal speleothems, especially in the vicinity of the access to the Blocks Hall.

Blocks Hall

As its name indicates, upon entering the Blocks Hall, the great quantity of large rocky blocks occupying the floor of the hall stands out, which we must navigate around to continue our visit.

Its dimensions are 48 x 25 x 4 m and it is formed by two levels: a lower level where clastic processes predominate, and an upper level, at whose southwestern end reconstructive processes predominate.

First we descend to the lower level where we can appreciate the abundant presence of banded gypsum blocks in gray and white colors, with a saccharoidal appearance, where the existence of a large isolated column stands out. Only at the southern end of this level can we see a small area with speleothems (stalagmites, stalactites, columns, and some eccentric formations).

Next we ascend again to move to the southwestern end of the Blocks Hall, where the most beautiful area of the cavity is found, as it is the most concretioned zone. In this area we can enjoy abundant flowstones, stalagmites, stalactites, rimstone pools, and curtains. The eccentric formations that cover the entire southwestern wall stand out for their singular beauty. Both to access and to exit this area, we must pass through a beautiful stalagmitic curtain that separates it from the blocks zone.

A warning that the cavity occasionally presents CO2 accumulations in the areas farthest from the entrance. During our visit to the cave on July 23, 2011, we had to abandon work earlier than planned.
